rationale: Replaces the undefined functional genus 'circuit-friendly signature verification algorithm' with 'EdDSA signature' while keeping the embedded-curve language at moderate breadth. EdDSA is a precisely defined signature scheme (RFC 8032, Bernstein et al. 2012) recognized in the art, so this is no longer an undefined functional characterization. The embedded-curve limitation naturally scopes to SNARK-compatible curves (Baby Jubjub, Jubjub, Bandersnatch, etc.) without resorting to undefined functional language. A separate new dependent claim can further narrow to Poseidon-based EdDSA on Baby Jubjub/BN128 as an Alice anchor if needed.
tradeoffs: Broader than candidate 01 (covers any EdDSA on any embedded curve, not just Baby Jubjub/BN128), which preserves more scope but could face a weaker enablement position if the examiner demands the specific curve. Narrower than the original genus since it excludes non-EdDSA schemes entirely.

alice_101.name: alice_101
alice_101.evidence: The claim recites concrete cryptographic operations: EdDSA signature verification on an embedded elliptic curve, Poseidon hash computation over four specific inputs (previous scope commitment, credential commitment, permission bitmask, expiry timestamp), all performed within a ZKP circuit. This is a specific machine transformation — circuit constraint generation for signature verification — not merely 'verify a signature on a computer.'
alice_101.critique: Still vulnerable to characterization as 'mathematical relationships organized on a computer.' The EdDSA verification and Poseidon hashing are known mathematical operations; an examiner citing Electric Power Group could argue the claim recites data gathering (collecting the four hash inputs) and mathematical analysis (hashing, signature checking) without a concrete improvement to computer functionality itself. The embedded-curve language helps tie to specific hardware-level constraint systems, but the claim doesn't recite the constraint topology (e.g., Num2Bits decomposition, scalar multiplication windows) that would make it read as circuit engineering rather than applied math. Scores mid-range: real mechanism, but not arity-specific or constraint-specific enough for top marks.

obviousness_103.name: obviousness_103
obviousness_103.evidence: Combining references is non-trivial: (1) Semaphore teaches Poseidon+EdDSA on Baby Jubjub for membership proofs but not delegation; (2) UCAN/Biscuit teach delegation chains with capability attenuation but in cleartext; (3) Tornado Cash Nova teaches chained Poseidon commitments with nullifiers but for value transfer not permission narrowing; (4) MACI teaches EdDSA Poseidon verification in Circom but for voting not delegation. The specific integration — EdDSA signature over a Poseidon hash of (scope_commitment, credential_commitment, permission_bitmask, expiry) inside a ZKP delegation circuit — requires combining at least 3-4 references with strained motivation.
obviousness_103.critique: An examiner would argue: MACI already demonstrates EdDSA-over-Poseidon-hash inside Circom circuits (element 1). UCAN teaches delegation tokens binding delegator identity to delegatee permissions with expiry (element 2). Tornado Nova teaches chaining Poseidon commitments across ZK proof hops (element 3). The motivation to combine is 'apply known ZK-friendly signature verification (MACI) to known delegation chain structure (UCAN) using known commitment chaining (Tornado).' The four-input Poseidon hash composition is a design choice, not a surprising technical result. KSR's 'finite number of identified, predictable solutions' argument applies. Scores moderate: real combination required, but each element is well-known in ZK literature.

support_112.name: support_112
support_112.evidence: The revision directly addresses the enablement weakness identified: replacing the undefined genus 'circuit-friendly signature verification algorithm' with the precisely defined 'EdDSA signature' (RFC 8032). EdDSA is a term of art with exact mathematical definition. The 'embedded in the scalar field of the proof system's base curve' is a well-understood constraint in SNARK circuit design. The four Poseidon hash inputs are each specifically defined in the spec. The claim language now matches what the specification actually discloses and enables.
support_112.critique: Minor residual risk: the claim covers EdDSA on ANY embedded curve (Baby Jubjub, Jubjub, Bandersnatch, etc.) but the specification likely only provides worked examples on Baby Jubjub/BN128. Under LizardTech, claiming a genus (all embedded curves) while disclosing only one species (Baby Jubjub) is risky if the examiner argues that different curves require materially different circuit implementations (different window sizes, different endomorphisms, different constraint counts). However, EdDSA verification is structurally identical across twisted Edwards curves differing only in constants, so this genus is likely adequately supported. The Poseidon hash arity (4 inputs) is concrete. Overall strong but not perfect — one worked example for a moderately broad genus.

design_around.name: design_around
design_around.evidence: The claim is tied specifically to EdDSA signatures. A competitor can substitute Schnorr signatures (which are trivially circuit-friendly on the same curves), ECDSA with circuit-optimized implementations (e.g., Spartan-ECDSA), or BLS signatures on pairing-friendly curves. The Poseidon hash of four specific inputs is more constraining, but reordering inputs, adding a domain separator, or using a different hash (Rescue, Griffin, Anemoi) escapes the Poseidon limitation even though the patent context uses Poseidon.
design_around.critique: EdDSA is one of several circuit-friendly signature schemes. The embedded-curve requirement is inherent to any SNARK-based signature verification, so it doesn't narrow the escape space. A competitor building the same product could: (1) use Schnorr instead of EdDSA on the same curve — hours of work; (2) use a different commitment structure (e.g., Pedersen commitment instead of Poseidon hash) — days of work; (3) hash 5 inputs instead of 4 by splitting one field — trivial. The claim's specificity to EdDSA is both its 112 strength and its design-around weakness. This is a low-to-moderate cost escape.

scope.name: scope
scope.evidence: The claim covers EdDSA on any embedded curve, which captures the primary implementation (Baby Jubjub/BN128) plus foreseeable alternatives (Jubjub/BLS12-381, Bandersnatch). The four-input Poseidon delegation token structure covers the main product architecture. As a dependent claim on claim 9, it adds specificity while the independent claim provides broader coverage.
scope.critique: The exclusion of non-EdDSA schemes is a real scope gap. As SNARK ecosystems mature, ECDSA-in-circuit (already demonstrated by 0xPARC's efficient-ECDSA) and lattice-based signatures may become commercially relevant for delegation. The claim also doesn't cover systems that use a 5-input or 3-input hash (e.g., omitting expiry from the hash and enforcing it separately). As a dependent claim this is acceptable — scope narrowing is expected — but it means this claim alone doesn't provide broad commercial coverage. It covers the primary product and one extension (alternative embedded curves) but not the full design space.

original_language: (d) verifying both the first zero-knowledge proof and the second zero-knowledge proof in a single blockchain transaction, wherein the on-chain verifier: (i) enforces that the session nonce embedded in each proof's public signals equals the transaction's session nonce argument, (ii) checks the session nonce for freshness against a used-nonce mapping, and (iii) writes the identity-bound scope commitment from the second zero-knowledge proof into a storage variable of the verifier contract indexed by the session nonce, the storage variable being subsequently read by a delegation verification function to enforce chain continuity; and
claim_text: (d) verifying both the first zero-knowledge proof and the second zero-knowledge proof in a single atomic verification operation performed by a verifier process that maintains a nonce-freshness store and a chain-state record, wherein the verifier process: (i) enforces that the session nonce embedded in each proof's public signals equals a session nonce argument supplied to the verification operation, (ii) checks the session nonce for freshness against the nonce-freshness store, and (iii) writes the identity-bound scope commitment from the second zero-knowledge proof into the chain-state record indexed by the session nonce, the chain-state record being subsequently read by a delegation verification function to enforce chain continuity; and
rationale: Replaces 'single blockchain transaction' and 'on-chain verifier' with the genus 'single atomic verification operation performed by a verifier process,' covering blockchain transactions, TEE-based verifiers, state channels, MPC committees, and centralized server verifiers alike. All functional constraints (nonce freshness, nonce equality, scope commitment write-through) are preserved. Existing Claim 2 (Groth16 + PLONK) remains valid as a dependent, and a new dependent can specify blockchain as a species. This closes the off-chain design-around identified by the attack for the broadest independent method claim.
tradeoffs: Broader genus may face additional prior art from off-chain ZKP verification systems. Prosecution may need to argue the combination of nonce-freshness store + chain-state record + identity-bound scope commitment write-through distinguishes over generic off-chain verifiers. Claim 16 (system claim) still recites 'blockchain-based identity registry contract' and would need separate broadening for full coverage.

alice_101.name: alice_101
alice_101.evidence: The revision replaces 'single blockchain transaction' and 'on-chain verifier' — concrete machine-tied language — with 'single atomic verification operation performed by a verifier process,' which is environment-agnostic and reads as a generic computer process performing data lookups and writes. Under Alice Step 2A Prong 1, the claim now recites: (1) comparing nonces (mental process / mathematical comparison), (2) checking freshness against a store (database lookup), (3) writing a value to a record indexed by nonce (conventional storage). The ZKP verification and identity-bound scope commitment language provide some technical grounding under Prong 2, but 'verifier process that maintains a nonce-freshness store and a chain-state record' is dangerously close to Electric Power Group's 'collecting, analyzing, and displaying data.' The original blockchain-specific language anchored this to a specific machine and transformation (on-chain state mutation); the genus version surrenders that anchor. An examiner citing BSG Tech would argue the nonce-freshness store and chain-state record are generic data-processing steps that any authentication system performs.
alice_101.critique: The broadening is directionally wrong for 101 survival. You traded your strongest Step 2B argument — immutable blockchain state transition as the specific machine — for scope. Consider keeping the independent claim blockchain-specific and adding a separate independent claim for the off-chain genus, or recite the dual-proof-system constraint (Groth16 + PLONK) in the independent claim to restore technical specificity.

obviousness_103.name: obviousness_103
obviousness_103.evidence: By broadening to any 'verifier process,' the prior art universe expands dramatically. The original claim required a blockchain verifier contract — a constrained execution environment with specific properties (deterministic execution, gas costs, storage slot semantics). The genus now admits: (a) any TLS/DTLS server performing mutual authentication with nonce freshness (RFC 5246 teaches nonce-freshness stores), (b) Aztec Connect's batched multi-proof verification with shared context, (c) any centralized ZKP verifier service with a nonce table and result cache. The combination becomes: Semaphore (ZKP membership proof) + any standard authentication protocol with nonce replay protection (IETF RFC 4949 defines nonce-freshness) + UCAN/Biscuit (delegation chain continuity via linked tokens). Motivation to combine: 'improve security of ZKP verification by adding standard replay protection.' The identity-bound scope commitment write-through for delegation chain continuity is the only non-trivial integration point, but Tornado Cash Nova teaches chained Poseidon commitments with state linkage across hops.
obviousness_103.critique: The scope commitment write-through linked to delegation verification is the load-bearing novelty, but it is now described so functionally ('chain-state record being subsequently read by a delegation verification function') that it reads as a design pattern rather than a specific technical mechanism. An examiner would combine Semaphore + RFC nonce replay protection + Tornado Nova chained commitments with KSR 'predictable use of prior art elements according to their established functions.'

support_112.name: support_112
support_112.evidence: The specification, per the abstract and background, describes blockchain-based registry with 'on-chain verifier,' 'storage variable of the verifier contract,' and 'single blockchain transaction.' The genus claims 'verifier process,' 'nonce-freshness store,' and 'chain-state record' — covering TEEs, state channels, MPC committees, and centralized servers. Under LizardTech v. Earth Resource Mapping, claiming a genus (any verifier process) when the spec discloses only one species (blockchain smart contract) is a written description deficiency. The spec would need to demonstrate possession of the full genus: how does a TEE-based verifier maintain chain-state records? How does a state channel enforce atomicity of dual-proof verification? The terms 'nonce-freshness store' and 'chain-state record' are newly coined abstractions of 'used-nonce mapping' and 'storage variable' — under Nautilus v. Biosig, are these terms defined with reasonable certainty, or are they just functional relabeling? An examiner would issue a 112(a) rejection for overclaiming genus with single-species disclosure and a 112(b) rejection requesting definitions of 'chain-state record' and 'nonce-freshness store.'
support_112.critique: Either add spec support for at least 2-3 alternative verification environments (TEE attestation-gated verifier, state channel verifier with dispute window, centralized HSM-backed verifier) or narrow the genus to 'verifier process executing in a trust-minimized environment' with spec-anchored definitions. As drafted, this claim will draw a LizardTech rejection in prosecution.

design_around.name: design_around
design_around.evidence: The revision successfully closes the off-chain verification escape route identified in the attack — a competitor cannot simply move verification off-chain to escape. However, new design-arounds emerge: (1) Non-atomic verification: perform the two ZKP verifications in separate operations with a linking token rather than a 'single atomic verification operation' — the claim requires atomicity, so two-step verification with cryptographic linking escapes. (2) Stateless verification: use a verifiable data structure (e.g., append-only log with inclusion proofs) instead of a 'nonce-freshness store' — the claim assumes stateful nonce tracking, so a stateless design with expiring proof windows escapes. (3) The claim still requires 'identity-bound scope commitment from the second zero-knowledge proof' — a system using attribute-based credentials without identity binding escapes. These are moderate-cost escapes (days of redesign work, not hours), representing genuine improvement over the original.
design_around.critique: The atomicity requirement is the weakest link. Consider 'in a verification session comprising one or more verification operations that collectively enforce nonce equality' to cover both atomic and multi-step verification. Also, 'nonce-freshness store' should be broadened to 'replay-prevention mechanism' to cover stateless approaches.

scope.name: scope
scope.evidence: The revision meaningfully expands commercial coverage from blockchain-only to any verification environment, which is strategically important as the industry moves toward hybrid on-chain/off-chain architectures (L2 rollups, TEE co-processors, intent-based systems). The claim now covers: blockchain smart contracts, TEE enclaves, centralized verification services, state channels, and MPC-based verifiers. This is a significant improvement over the original. However, (1) Claim 16 (system claim) still recites 'blockchain-based identity registry contract' per the candidate's own admission, leaving a gap in the system claims. (2) The claim does not cover asynchronous verification patterns (common in L2 rollups with delayed finality). (3) The claim's requirement for 'single atomic verification operation' excludes optimistic verification patterns where proofs are posted and challenged rather than eagerly verified. These are commercially relevant deployment patterns that escape coverage.
scope.critique: Good directional improvement but incomplete. Need corresponding broadening of Claim 16 system claim and consideration of asynchronous/optimistic verification patterns. A CIP should cover recursive SNARK aggregation of the dual proofs, which would be a natural extension.

original_language: (a) performing a mutual authentication handshake between a human user and an AI agent by verifying, in a single blockchain transaction, a first zero-knowledge proof of the human user's Merkle membership in a human identity tree and a second zero-knowledge proof of the AI agent's Merkle membership in an agent credential tree, wherein the on-chain verifier enforces that a session nonce embedded in each proof's public signals equals a transaction-argument session nonce
claim_text: (a) performing a mutual authentication handshake between a human user and an AI agent by verifying, in a single atomic verification operation performed by a verifier process, a first zero-knowledge proof of the human user's Merkle membership in a human identity tree and a second zero-knowledge proof of the AI agent's Merkle membership in an agent credential tree, wherein the verifier process enforces that a session nonce embedded in each proof's public signals equals a verification-argument session nonce
rationale: Claim 15 is identified in the attack as the narrowest, hardest-to-design-around independent claim, yet it still requires a 'single blockchain transaction.' This substitution generalizes to 'single atomic verification operation performed by a verifier process,' capturing off-chain deployments (TEE, state channel, server) while preserving all downstream constraints in steps (b)-(d). Combined with Candidate 1 on Claim 1, this closes the off-chain gap across both the core handshake claim and the integrated method claim.
tradeoffs: Steps (b)-(d) of Claim 15 still reference 'on-chain chain-state record' and 'verifier contract' — these would ideally be updated to 'chain-state record' and 'verifier process' for full consistency, but are arguably construed as features of the verifier process introduced in step (a). Claim 15A dependent still anchors to the specific blockchain+Groth16+PLONK stack as an Alice safety net.

alice_101.name: alice_101
alice_101.evidence: The revision replaces 'single blockchain transaction' with 'single atomic verification operation performed by a verifier process.' This abstracts away the only concrete machine-transformation anchor (blockchain) and replaces it with functional language ('verifier process,' 'atomic verification operation') that reads as pure software abstraction. The claim now recites: verify two ZK proofs atomically with matching nonces. Under Electric Power Group, 'collecting, analyzing, and displaying data' in a generic computing environment is abstract. A 'verifier process' performing an 'atomic verification operation' is exactly the kind of result-oriented functional claiming Alice Step 1 targets. The original blockchain anchor at least tied to a specific technological environment; removing it weakens 101 posture significantly. The Merkle membership and ZK proof recitations add some technical flavor, but without hardware or specific circuit constraints in this step, an examiner would characterize this as 'verify credentials using math on a generic computer.'
alice_101.critique: Dangerous trade: you gained scope but surrendered your strongest 101 anchor. The blockchain transaction was a specific machine-transformation that grounded the claim. 'Verifier process' is so generic it could be a Python script on a laptop. Consider adding back at least one concrete constraint — e.g., 'wherein the atomic verification operation comprises pairing-based elliptic curve verification of the first proof and polynomial commitment verification of the second proof' — to maintain technical specificity.

obviousness_103.name: obviousness_103
obviousness_103.evidence: The core novelty — dual-population mutual authentication with session nonce equality across heterogeneous ZK proofs — survives the revision. An examiner would still need to combine Semaphore (human Merkle membership), Iden3 or a similar agent credential system, Aztec Connect (batched multi-proof verification with shared context), and the motivation to enforce cross-proof nonce equality for mutual authentication. The generalization to 'verifier process' does not materially change the combination analysis since the novelty was never in the blockchain per se but in the dual-tree mutual handshake pattern. However, removing 'blockchain transaction' slightly lowers the bar because off-chain verification of multiple ZK proofs with shared context is more conventional in TEE and MPC literature (e.g., standard TLS-like handshake patterns with ZK), potentially reducing the combination to 2-3 references with clearer motivation.
obviousness_103.critique: The off-chain generalization opens a new combination path: standard server-side ZK verification (any SNARK library) + Semaphore membership + session tokens. Server-side dual-proof verification with shared nonces is closer to conventional authentication middleware patterns than on-chain batch verification was. You've made the examiner's job slightly easier on 103.

support_112.name: support_112
support_112.evidence: The specification as described in the abstract and background consistently refers to 'on-chain' verification, 'blockchain-based registry,' and 'single on-chain transaction.' The revision introduces 'verifier process' and 'atomic verification operation' — neither term appears in the provided specification text. Under Ariad v. Eli Lilly, the specification must demonstrate possession of the full scope claimed. If the spec only discloses blockchain-based on-chain verification as the embodiment, claiming genus coverage over TEEs, state channels, and server-side verification without disclosing those embodiments risks a written description rejection. 'Atomic verification operation' is also potentially indefinite under Nautilus — what makes a verification 'atomic' in a non-transactional (non-blockchain) context? The spec likely defines atomicity through blockchain transaction semantics, not generic process semantics.
support_112.critique: This is the revision's Achilles' heel. You cannot claim what the spec does not possess. If the full specification only describes blockchain transactions as the verification context, an examiner will reject under 112(a) for overclaiming. You need either: (1) amend the spec in a CIP/non-provisional to add TEE and state-channel embodiments before filing this claim language, or (2) keep the blockchain anchor and add dependent claims for off-chain variants with new spec support. Filing this claim language against a blockchain-only spec is a gift to the examiner.

design_around.name: design_around
design_around.evidence: The generalization to 'verifier process' closes the most obvious escape route (move verification off-chain to a server or TEE). A competitor can no longer avoid infringement simply by running the same dual-proof verification in a centralized backend instead of on-chain. However, escapes remain: (1) use a single unified proof system instead of two heterogeneous proofs (one circuit that proves both human membership and agent credential simultaneously), avoiding the 'first proof' and 'second proof' structure; (2) use sequential verification rather than 'single atomic operation' — verify human proof, issue session token, then verify agent proof in a separate call; (3) use a different binding mechanism than session nonce equality (e.g., commitment to a shared secret derived from a DH exchange rather than a transaction-argument nonce).
design_around.critique: The sequential-verification escape is cheap (days, not weeks) and preserves the same security properties. The unified-proof escape requires moderate circuit redesign but is architecturally cleaner anyway. The nonce-binding escape is trivial. Net: you closed one escape but the claim structure still has multiple moderate-cost exits.

scope.name: scope
scope.evidence: The revision significantly broadens commercial coverage. The original claim was limited to blockchain deployments; the revised claim covers: on-chain smart contract verification, off-chain server verification, TEE-based verification, state channel verification, L2 rollup verification, and hybrid architectures. This captures the primary product (on-chain mutual auth), the most obvious extension (off-chain/hybrid), and foreseeable enterprise deployments that may prefer server-side ZK verification without blockchain overhead. The claim now covers the realistic commercial landscape where many enterprise customers will want the same cryptographic guarantees without blockchain infrastructure costs.
scope.critique: Good scope expansion in principle, but it's only commercially valuable if 112 support exists. A broad claim that gets rejected for lack of written description has zero commercial coverage. The scope gain is real but contingent on spec support that appears to be missing. Also note: steps (b)-(d) still reference 'on-chain chain-state record' and 'verifier contract,' creating internal inconsistency that narrows effective scope back toward blockchain deployments despite step (a)'s generalization.

original_language: (a) maintaining, on a blockchain, a Lean Incremental Merkle Tree storing agent credential commitments and a session-indexed on-chain chain-state record, the chain-state record comprising a mapping from session nonce to a field-element commitment, wherein the chain-state record is written under exactly two conditions: by a handshake verification function upon successful mutual handshake verification (to seed the chain with an identity-bound scope commitment output by the handshake's agent proof), and by the delegation verification function upon successful delegation proof verification (to advance the chain by one hop);
claim_text: (a) maintaining, in a verifier-accessible data store, a Lean Incremental Merkle Tree storing agent credential commitments and a session-indexed chain-state record, the chain-state record comprising a mapping from session nonce to a field-element commitment, wherein the chain-state record is written under exactly two conditions: by a handshake verification function upon successful mutual handshake verification (to seed the chain with an identity-bound scope commitment output by the handshake's agent proof), and by the delegation verification function upon successful delegation proof verification (to advance the chain by one hop);
rationale: Claim 9 is the independent delegation method claim, and its step (a) anchors the entire delegation chain to 'on a blockchain.' Replacing with 'in a verifier-accessible data store' and dropping 'on-chain' from 'chain-state record' covers off-chain delegation verification — the most commercially critical gap since AI agent orchestration pipelines (the primary delegation use case) rarely touch blockchains. The two-condition write invariant and all downstream steps (b)-(i) remain intact as functional constraints.
tradeoffs: Step 9(i) still contains references to 'on-chain chain-state mapping value' and 'on-chain constant' that would need conforming amendments for full internal consistency. The broadened genus 'verifier-accessible data store' is very wide and may invite §112 indefiniteness challenges if not supported by specification disclosure of off-chain embodiments — fortunately Alternative Embodiment 5 in the spec discloses this mode explicitly.

alice_101.name: alice_101
alice_101.evidence: Claim still recites Lean Incremental Merkle Tree, field-element commitments, session nonce mappings, two-condition write invariant, and identity-bound scope commitments from handshake proofs — concrete cryptographic mechanism, not purely abstract.
alice_101.critique: Replacing 'on a blockchain' with 'in a verifier-accessible data store' removes the most specific machine-or-transformation anchor. 'Verifier-accessible data store' reads closer to generic computer storage, weakening the Alice step-two argument. An examiner citing Electric Power Group would note that storing a mapping in a generic data store under two conditions is closer to 'organizing human activity' or 'mathematical relationships' than the blockchain-specific embodiment was. The remaining technical specificity (Merkle tree, field-element commitment, delegation proof) still carries weight, but the broadened storage genus pulls toward abstraction.

obviousness_103.name: obviousness_103
obviousness_103.evidence: The two-condition write invariant (handshake-seeded chain, delegation-advanced chain) with identity-bound scope commitments still requires combining Semaphore (Merkle membership), UCAN/Biscuit (capability attenuation), and Tornado Nova (chained Poseidon commitments) — at minimum three references with strained motivation.
obviousness_103.critique: Moving from blockchain to generic data store actually makes the combination slightly more obvious: off-chain verification of ZK proofs in a database is well-understood (every L2 rollup sequencer does this). The novel integration pattern — handshake-seeding into delegation chain advancement — remains the primary 103 defense, but the broadened storage medium adds no novelty itself. A KSR 'obvious to try' argument is slightly easier when the storage medium is unconstrained.

support_112.name: support_112
support_112.evidence: Alternative Embodiment 5 allegedly discloses off-chain verification mode, providing some written description support for the broadened genus. The candidate itself acknowledges step 9(i) still references 'on-chain chain-state mapping value' and 'on-chain constant.'
support_112.critique: Fatal internal inconsistency: step (a) now claims 'verifier-accessible data store' (genus) while step (i) of the same claim still recites 'on-chain' (species). Under Nautilus v. Biosig, a POSITA cannot determine with reasonable certainty whether the claim requires blockchain or not — classic indefiniteness. Additionally, 'verifier-accessible data store' is an extremely broad genus. Under LizardTech, if the spec only works through blockchain + one off-chain embodiment, claiming the entire genus of 'verifier-accessible data stores' (which includes SGX enclaves, TEEs, centralized databases, P2P DHTs, etc.) likely exceeds what the inventor possessed. The single Alternative Embodiment 5 may be insufficient species to support this genus breadth under Ariad v. Eli Lilly.

design_around.name: design_around
design_around.evidence: The revision closes the most commercially relevant escape route: moving delegation verification off-chain. Competitors building AI agent orchestration pipelines (which overwhelmingly operate off-chain) can no longer trivially escape by avoiding blockchain settlement.
design_around.critique: The escape narrows but does not disappear. A competitor can still avoid the two-condition write invariant by using a single unified verification function (not separate handshake-seed and delegation-advance conditions), or by not using session-indexed chain-state records at all (e.g., stateless delegation verification where each proof is self-contained). The Lean Incremental Merkle Tree requirement is also escapable — a standard sparse Merkle tree or accumulator-based approach avoids this element. Moderate redesign cost but achievable in days.

scope.name: scope
scope.evidence: Broadening from blockchain to 'verifier-accessible data store' captures the dominant commercial deployment topology: off-chain AI agent orchestration with optional on-chain settlement. This covers cloud-based agent platforms, enterprise middleware, and hybrid architectures.
scope.critique: Good commercial expansion, but the internal inconsistency with step 9(i) means the claim as-drafted may actually be construed as still requiring on-chain elements, potentially negating the intended broadening. If the examiner construes 'on-chain' in 9(i) as limiting, the scope gain evaporates. Additionally, the broadened genus may be so wide that it invites 112 rejections that force narrowing amendments, ultimately returning scope to near-original levels. The scope is aspirational until the 112 issues are resolved.

original_language: by updating the revoked agent's credential commitment to a designated invalid value in the second Merkle tree via an incremental tree update operation
claim_text: by updating the revoked agent's credential commitment to zero in the second Merkle tree via an incremental tree update operation
rationale: Directly matches the specification's sole disclosure in Section 4.4, Step 4: 'credential commitment is set to zero in the agentTree.' This eliminates the indefiniteness rejection under Nautilus v. Biosig by replacing the open-ended 'designated invalid value' with the concrete value 'zero' that the specification supports. A POSITA can now determine with reasonable certainty the exact scope of the claim.
tradeoffs: Narrows scope to zero only; a competitor using a different sentinel (e.g., hash of a revocation flag) would design around this claim. However, the specification discloses only zero, so broader language was unsupported anyway.

alice_101.name: alice_101
alice_101.evidence: The limitation recites a concrete technical operation — updating a specific value (zero) in a specific data structure (Merkle tree) via a specific mechanism (incremental tree update). This is not purely abstract; it ties to a defined data-structure transformation. However, 'set a field to zero to invalidate it' is a well-understood, routine, and conventional (WURC) database operation dressed in Merkle tree clothing. Under Electric Power Group and BSG Tech, the Merkle tree context adds some technical specificity but the core act of zeroing a record for revocation is generic.
alice_101.critique: The revision neither helps nor hurts 101 meaningfully. The original 'designated invalid value' was equally concrete as a technical step; narrowing to 'zero' does not add a new technical integration or transformation. A hostile examiner would argue this is conventional data invalidation applied to a known data structure.

obviousness_103.name: obviousness_103
obviousness_103.evidence: Setting a revoked entry to zero is the single most obvious sentinel choice in computer science — it is the default null/empty/uninitialized value in virtually every programming language and every sparse Merkle tree implementation. Semaphore v4's LeanIMT initializes empty leaves to zero; Tornado Cash's Merkle trees use zero-valued leaves for empty slots. A POSITA combining (1) any ZKP credential tree (Iden3, Semaphore) with (2) standard revocation-by-tree-update would arrive at 'set to zero' without inventive effort. Under KSR, the motivation to combine is explicit: zero is the identity element of Poseidon's field and the natural 'nothing here' marker.
obviousness_103.critique: The candidate's own tradeoffs section concedes the specification discloses only zero, which is precisely because zero is the obvious choice, not because it is inventive. Two references (Semaphore + any revocation scheme) combine trivially.

support_112.name: support_112
support_112.evidence: The revision directly quotes the specification Section 4.4, Step 4: 'credential commitment is set to zero in the agentTree.' This is a textbook cure for indefiniteness under Nautilus v. Biosig — replacing an open-ended term ('designated invalid value') with the precise value the specification discloses. The claim scope now exactly matches the enabled embodiment. No Ariad genus/species problem remains because the claim and spec are coextensive.
support_112.critique: This is the one dimension where the revision clearly succeeds. The only minor deduction is that the spec apparently discloses a single embodiment (zero only), so under LizardTech there is no genus support — but the candidate wisely claims only the species, which is proper.

design_around.name: design_around
design_around.evidence: A competitor escapes by using literally any non-zero sentinel value: 1, type(uint256).max, Poseidon('revoked'), keccak256('REVOKED_AGENT'), or a hash of a revocation nonce. This is a one-line constant change — replace 0 with another value in the tree update function. The Merkle tree operations, circuit constraints, and verification logic remain identical in every other respect. The candidate's own rationale concedes this: 'a competitor using a different sentinel (e.g., hash of a revocation flag) would design around this claim.'
design_around.critique: This is a fatal weakness. The claim literally protects only the value zero. Any competent engineer reading this patent would use a non-zero sentinel and be completely clear of infringement. The specification's failure to disclose alternative sentinels means the applicant cannot broaden this limitation without new matter.

scope.name: scope
scope.evidence: The claim covers exactly one sentinel value (zero) for exactly one revocation mechanism (incremental Merkle tree update) in exactly one tree (the agent credential tree). It does not cover: alternative sentinels, removal-based revocation (deleting the leaf rather than overwriting), accumulator-based revocation (RSA accumulators, CL accumulators), revocation lists, epoch-based credential expiry, or any revocation mechanism that does not use an incremental tree update. The commercial coverage is limited to the narrow case where a competitor copies the exact zero-sentinel approach.
scope.critique: The revision trades all scope for 112 compliance — a textbook overcorrection. The original 'designated invalid value' at least covered alternative sentinels (even if indefinite). Now the claim is definite but commercially irrelevant because the design-around cost is minutes, not days.

original_language: by updating the revoked agent's credential commitment to a designated invalid value in the second Merkle tree via an incremental tree update operation that changes the tree root, such that proofs generated against pre-revocation roots verify only while those roots remain in the root history, and proofs generated against post-revocation roots fail Merkle membership verification due to the invalidated leaf value
claim_text: by updating the revoked agent's credential commitment to a value of zero in the second Merkle tree via an incremental tree update operation that changes the tree root, wherein zero is not producible by the five-input Poseidon hash computation that generates valid agent credential commitments, such that proofs generated against pre-revocation roots verify only while those roots remain in the root history, and proofs generated against post-revocation roots fail Merkle membership verification because the zero leaf value does not match any credential commitment derivable from valid agent credential fields
rationale: Replaces 'designated invalid value' with 'zero' and adds the technical rationale why zero is invalid — it is not in the range of the Poseidon5 credential commitment function. This not only cures the indefiniteness but strengthens the claim by tying the invalidity to the cryptographic construction, making the revocation mechanism's correctness self-evident from the claim language. Addresses the reviewer's alternative suggestion of explaining why zero qualifies as invalid.
tradeoffs: Adds specificity that could theoretically be designed around if a system used a different hash function where zero is in range. Also slightly longer claim language. But the added structural explanation strengthens the 112(a) enablement position.

alice_101.name: alice_101
alice_101.evidence: Recites concrete cryptographic mechanism: zero leaf value, five-input Poseidon hash, incremental Merkle tree update, root history window. Ties invalidity to a specific property of the hash function's range rather than abstract 'invalidation.'
alice_101.critique: Still fundamentally describes data structure manipulation (set leaf to zero, check membership) — the 'zero is not in the range of Poseidon5' observation is a mathematical property of the hash, not a novel machine transformation. An examiner could characterize this as 'conventional revocation (nullify a record) applied to a Merkle tree on a generic computer.' The root history staleness mechanism is taught by Tornado MerkleTreeWithHistory. Saves itself from sub-10 only because the five-input arity and range argument are specific enough to resist the 'apply it' characterization.

obviousness_103.name: obviousness_103
obviousness_103.evidence: Setting a revoked entry to a sentinel value (zero/null) in an authenticated data structure is standard practice. Tornado Cash Nova already chains Poseidon commitments with on-chain nullifiers; Semaphore v4 uses incremental Merkle trees with Poseidon. The root history window is taught by Tornado MerkleTreeWithHistory.
obviousness_103.critique: A PHOSITA would combine: (1) any Merkle-tree credential system (Iden3, Semaphore) + (2) standard revocation-by-leaf-zeroing (a pattern from certificate revocation and sparse Merkle trees, e.g., Iden3's revocation tree) + (3) the trivial observation that Poseidon with non-trivial multi-field input cannot produce zero. Motivation to combine is clear — you need revocation, zeroing is the simplest approach, and the range argument is an inherent property the implementer would notice. Two-reference combination with obvious motivation keeps this in the 5-9 band.

support_112.name: support_112
support_112.evidence: The revision directly cures the indefiniteness of 'designated invalid value' by specifying 'zero' and providing the cryptographic rationale (not producible by five-input Poseidon). The spec presumably discloses the five-field agent credential commitment (model hash, operator pubkey, etc.) and the Poseidon hash construction, which enables a PHOSITA to verify the range claim.
support_112.critique: Strong improvement. The only residual risk is whether the specification explicitly states that zero is outside the Poseidon5 range or merely implies it from the hash construction. If the spec only describes the five-input commitment without an explicit worked proof that H(x1..x5)≠0 for valid inputs, an examiner could push back on whether possession of the full scope is shown. Minor issue — this is mathematically verifiable from the Poseidon specification itself, so a 112(a) rejection would be weak.

design_around.name: design_around
design_around.evidence: The claim is pinned to 'zero' as the sentinel value. A competitor escapes by using any other value outside the Poseidon5 range (e.g., 1, or a fixed constant), or by using a separate revocation bitmap/accumulator alongside the credential tree, or by using nullifier-based revocation (publish a nullifier derived from the revoked credential) instead of modifying the leaf.
design_around.critique: The specificity that strengthens 112 directly weakens design-around resistance. Swapping 'zero' for '1' or any other non-range value is a trivial code change. More substantively, a competitor could avoid leaf modification entirely by maintaining a separate sparse Merkle tree of revoked credentials (Iden3's approach) or an on-chain revocation bitmap — architecturally different but functionally identical. Hours of work, not days.

scope.name: scope
scope.evidence: Covers the specific zero-sentinel revocation mechanism with root history window for the agent credential tree. This is one valid commercial embodiment of revocation.
scope.critique: Narrow to a single revocation mechanism (leaf zeroing) with a single sentinel value (zero). Does not cover: revocation via separate accumulator, revocation via nullifier publication, revocation via credential expiry, or revocation via bitmap. A product using any alternative revocation architecture is outside scope. Covers the primary product's approach but no extensions or alternatives.

original_language: by updating the revoked agent's credential commitment to a designated invalid value in the second Merkle tree via an incremental tree update operation that changes the tree root, such that proofs generated against pre-revocation roots verify only while those roots remain in the root history, and proofs generated against post-revocation roots fail Merkle membership verification due to the invalidated leaf value
claim_text: by updating the revoked agent's credential commitment to a value outside the range of the Poseidon hash function used to compute valid credential commitments in the second Merkle tree via an incremental tree update operation that changes the tree root, wherein the value outside the range is zero, such that proofs generated against pre-revocation roots verify only while those roots remain in the root history, and proofs generated against post-revocation roots fail Merkle membership verification because the replacement leaf value cannot be produced by the credential commitment computation from any set of valid credential fields
rationale: Uses genus-species structure: the genus is 'a value outside the range of the Poseidon hash function used to compute valid credential commitments' (definite because a POSITA can determine whether any given value is in the range of Poseidon5), and the species is 'zero.' This preserves slightly broader scope than pure narrowing to zero while curing indefiniteness — the claim now provides a clear criterion ('outside the range of the Poseidon hash') that a POSITA can evaluate with reasonable certainty per Nautilus.
tradeoffs: The genus 'value outside the range of the Poseidon hash function' is broader than just zero but requires proving that such values exist and are identifiable by a POSITA. An examiner could argue the range of Poseidon over BN128 is not trivially characterized, though zero is widely accepted as outside it. The species 'zero' anchors the claim if the genus is challenged.

alice_101.name: alice_101
alice_101.evidence: The claim recites concrete cryptographic primitives: Poseidon hash function, Merkle tree with incremental update, root history mechanism, and a specific mathematical property (replacement value outside hash range). These are specific technical operations, not mere 'apply it on a computer' recitations. The transformation is concrete: leaf replacement changes the tree root, causing subsequent membership proofs to fail.
alice_101.critique: However, the underlying concept — revoking a credential by marking it invalid in a data structure — is well-understood, routine, and conventional. An examiner citing Electric Power Group could argue this is merely 'collecting, analyzing, and displaying data' about credential validity. The Poseidon-specific language adds technical texture but the operative logic (set leaf to invalid value → proofs fail) is a straightforward application of known Merkle tree semantics. No specific circuit constraints, gate counts, or hardware-level operations are recited. Sits solidly in the 'concrete mechanism but not deeply technical' band.

obviousness_103.name: obviousness_103
obviousness_103.evidence: Setting a Merkle leaf to zero for revocation is standard practice. Tornado Cash's MerkleTreeWithHistory (2019) teaches circular root history buffers and staleness-based invalidation. Semaphore v4 teaches Poseidon-based Merkle trees with incremental updates. Any practitioner building a ZKP credential system would trivially combine these: (1) Semaphore's Poseidon Merkle tree, (2) Tornado's root history buffer, (3) the well-known technique of zeroing a leaf to revoke membership.
obviousness_103.critique: The 'genus' of 'value outside the range of the Poseidon hash function' is a thin abstraction over the obvious choice of zero. A POSITA would immediately recognize that any value not producible by the commitment hash function serves as an invalidation marker, and zero is the canonical choice. The motivation to combine these references is clear and strong under KSR: all three references operate in the same ZKP-over-Merkle-tree domain, solving the same revocation problem. No unexpected technical result is produced — the system behaves exactly as one would predict.

support_112.name: support_112
support_112.evidence: The species (zero) is well-supported and definite. The genus-species structure is proper claim drafting practice, and the species anchors enablement if the genus is challenged. The mechanical steps (incremental tree update, root history, proof failure) are standard Merkle tree operations that a POSITA can implement.
support_112.critique: The genus — 'value outside the range of the Poseidon hash function' — raises enablement and definiteness concerns. Poseidon maps F_p^n → F_p over the BN128 scalar field. Characterizing the exact image of Poseidon is a non-trivial mathematical question. The spec must demonstrate that a POSITA can determine with 'reasonable certainty' (Nautilus v. Biosig) whether a given field element is in Poseidon's range. Zero is widely assumed to be outside it, but the spec likely does not contain a formal proof of this. An examiner under LizardTech could argue the genus claims a broader set ('any out-of-range value') while only enabling one species (zero), and the spec does not show possession of the genus. Additionally, the phrase 'valid credential fields' introduces potential indefiniteness — what constitutes 'valid' is not bounded by the claim language alone.

design_around.name: design_around
design_around.evidence: The claim is limited to one specific revocation mechanism: leaf replacement with out-of-range values in the same Merkle tree.
design_around.critique: A competitor escapes trivially by using any of several well-known alternative revocation mechanisms: (1) a separate revocation bitmap or accumulator (costs hours to implement, completely avoids leaf replacement), (2) nullifier-based revocation where the revoked agent's nullifier is published to a revocation set (no tree modification needed), (3) epoch-based credential rotation where credentials expire and are re-issued to non-revoked agents, (4) a sparse Merkle tree with a separate 'revoked' subtree. None of these approaches touch the claimed mechanism. Even within the leaf-replacement paradigm, a competitor could replace the leaf with a valid-looking but unclaimed commitment (e.g., hash of a known-unused secret) rather than an 'out-of-range value,' sidestepping the claim entirely.

scope.name: scope
scope.evidence: The claim covers the primary revocation embodiment (zero-leaf replacement) and extends via the genus to other out-of-range sentinel values. It captures the core product's revocation flow.
scope.critique: Scope is narrow to a single revocation mechanism within a single Merkle tree architecture. It does not cover: accumulator-based revocation, nullifier-based revocation, credential expiry, re-issuance models, or any revocation mechanism that doesn't involve replacing a leaf with an out-of-range value. The genus adds marginal breadth (other out-of-range values beyond zero), but since the escape routes are architectural rather than parametric, this additional breadth provides negligible commercial coverage. A competitor building the same overall system simply uses a different revocation primitive.

original_language: such that the commitment cannot be reproduced by any party not in possession of both fields. An actor with the same permission bits but a different credential cannot produce a matching identity-bound scope commitment, and therefore cannot satisfy the chain-linking check in the Delegation circuit (Section 5). This prevents impersonation attacks in delegation chains.
claim_text: such that a valid zero-knowledge proof of knowledge of the pre-image requires the prover to demonstrate both possession of the policy field and Merkle membership of the identity field in an on-chain identity tree, thereby preventing any party who does not hold a Merkle-included credential commitment from satisfying the chain-linking constraint in the Delegation circuit (Section 5) even if that party can compute the hash value offline by enumerating policy field values. The identity-binding property is enforced not by the computational hardness of inverting the hash, but by the conjunction of the hash pre-image constraint and the Merkle membership constraint within the zero-knowledge proof circuit: a delegation prover must simultaneously demonstrate that its credential commitment is a leaf in the on-chain agent credential tree and that the Poseidon hash of its permission bitmask and credential commitment equals the previous scope commitment recorded on-chain. An actor who computes the hash offline but whose credential commitment is not Merkle-included in the agent credential tree cannot produce a valid delegation proof, and therefore cannot impersonate the delegator in a delegation chain.
rationale: This is the root-cause fix. The original definition claimed hash pre-image hardness ('cannot be reproduced'), which Section 3.8 itself contradicts by disclosing 2^64 enumeration feasibility. The revised definition honestly locates the security guarantee in the ZK proof's Merkle membership constraint rather than in computational hardness of the hash. This eliminates the Interval Licensing v. AOL inconsistency by making the spec definition and the Section 3.8 disclosure logically compatible — both now agree that offline hash reproduction is possible, but the ZK proof circuit prevents exploitation.
tradeoffs: The revised definition is longer and more technical, which may invite additional scrutiny from examiners. It also makes explicit that the hash alone is not the security barrier, which an adversary could cite in a narrowing argument — but this honesty is required to avoid indefiniteness.

alice_101.name: alice_101
alice_101.evidence: The revised language recites concrete cryptographic mechanism: Poseidon hash pre-image constraint conjuncted with Merkle membership constraint within a zero-knowledge proof circuit, on-chain agent credential tree, specific circuit-level enforcement. This is not 'apply ZK on a computer' — it ties to specific machine transformations (circuit constraint satisfaction, Merkle path verification).
alice_101.critique: Still somewhat result-oriented in places ('preventing any party who does not hold a Merkle-included credential commitment'). The identity-binding property is described as an emergent property of two constraints rather than as a specific circuit topology with gate-level detail. An examiner could argue the conjunction of 'hash pre-image check + Merkle check' is a well-understood combination (Tornado Cash, Semaphore both do this), and framing it as a security property rather than a specific technical step edges toward abstract idea territory. Does not reach 15+ because it lacks arity-specific hash parameterization or explicit circuit constraint references (Num2Bits, LessThan).

obviousness_103.name: obviousness_103
obviousness_103.evidence: Requires combining at least three references: (1) Semaphore for Merkle membership proofs with Poseidon, (2) Tornado Cash Nova for chained Poseidon commitments with on-chain nullifiers, (3) UCAN/Biscuit for capability attenuation semantics — plus the non-obvious integration insight that the security guarantee lives in the proof-circuit conjunction rather than hash hardness. No single reference teaches identity-bound scope commitments where security derives from the conjunction of hash pre-image and Merkle membership within a delegation-specific ZK circuit.
obviousness_103.critique: A PHOSITA in ZK systems would recognize that 'prove you know the pre-image AND prove that one input is a Merkle leaf' is a standard circuit composition pattern — Tornado Cash Nova already chains commitments where the commitment includes a secret that must be Merkle-included. The motivation to combine Merkle membership with hash pre-image knowledge in a single proof is well-established in the mixer literature. The specific application to delegation chains adds some distance, but the underlying mechanism (conjunction of constraints in a SNARK) is the standard way ZK circuits compose security properties. Stops short of 15 because the mechanism is an application of known ZK composition techniques to a new domain.

support_112.name: support_112
support_112.evidence: This revision directly addresses and eliminates the Interval Licensing v. AOL inconsistency — the original spec said the hash 'cannot be reproduced' while Section 3.8 disclosed 2^64 enumeration feasibility. The revised language honestly and precisely locates the security guarantee in the ZK proof circuit's conjunction of constraints rather than computational hardness. Both the definition and Section 3.8 now agree: offline hash reproduction is possible, but exploitation is prevented by the Merkle membership requirement enforced within the proof. Key terms ('identity-bound scope commitment', 'chain-linking constraint') are now defined with mechanistic precision.
support_112.critique: The increased length and technical density introduce new definiteness risks: 'Merkle-included credential commitment' must be consistently used throughout the spec (check all 23+ occurrences). The phrase 'enumerating policy field values' implicitly concedes a narrow bitmask space — an examiner might demand specification of the exact bitmask width and ask whether the enablement extends to larger bitmask spaces where enumeration is infeasible. The two-paragraph explanation, while thorough, could be read as admitting the original mechanism was weaker than claimed, potentially creating prosecution history estoppel. Does not reach 18+ because the genus (any conjunction of hash pre-image + Merkle membership) may exceed the single worked example (Poseidon + LeanIMT).

design_around.name: design_around
design_around.evidence: The revised language specifically requires Merkle membership in an on-chain identity tree as the binding mechanism. A competitor must use some form of set membership proof conjuncted with a hash pre-image proof to infringe. Switching to a fundamentally different binding mechanism (e.g., BBS+ signature-based credential presentation, RSA accumulator membership, or polynomial commitment-based set membership) would require days of circuit redesign.
design_around.critique: The revision narrows the security definition to Merkle-tree-specific binding, which actually WIDENS the design-around surface compared to the original vague 'cannot be reproduced' language. A competitor could: (1) use a KZG polynomial commitment for set membership instead of Merkle trees — same security property, escapes 'Merkle membership' language; (2) use BBS+ blind signatures where the issuer attests credential validity rather than proving tree membership; (3) use a Verkle tree (not a Merkle tree) for the identity registry. Each escape requires moderate engineering effort (days, not weeks) but preserves the same product functionality. The honest admission that hash hardness isn't the barrier also tells competitors exactly what IS the barrier, making it easier to route around.

scope.name: scope
scope.evidence: The revision supports claims 1, 5, 9, and 15 — covering mutual authentication, scope commitments, delegation chains, and the combined system. The identity-binding mechanism described is fundamental to the delegation chain security model, so strengthening this definition strengthens the entire delegation claim family. The language covers both human and AI agent populations through the generic 'credential commitment' and 'identity tree' references.
scope.critique: The specificity to Poseidon hash and Merkle trees limits commercial coverage. Alternative ZK-friendly hash functions (Rescue, Griffin, Anemoi) and alternative set membership primitives (RSA accumulators, Verkle trees, polynomial commitments) are commercially viable and not covered by this language. The revision also does not extend to future CIP candidates like recursive SNARK verification where the Merkle membership proof could be folded into an IVC chain rather than verified independently. Covers the primary product implementation and obvious Merkle-tree variants, but leaves substantial room for alternative implementations that achieve identical security properties through different cryptographic primitives.

original_language: outputting, as a public signal, an identity-bound scope commitment computed as a Poseidon hash of the permission bitmask and the AI agent's credential commitment that is Merkle-included in the second Merkle tree, without revealing the AI agent's credential fields
claim_text: outputting, as a public signal, an identity-bound scope commitment computed as a Poseidon hash of the permission bitmask and the AI agent's credential commitment that is Merkle-included in the second Merkle tree, wherein the identity-bound scope commitment is identity-bound by virtue of the zero-knowledge proof circuit simultaneously constraining both (i) that the credential commitment component of the Poseidon hash pre-image is a leaf in the second Merkle tree via a Merkle membership proof computed within the circuit, and (ii) that the permission bitmask component of the Poseidon hash pre-image satisfies the required scope policy via bit-by-bit enforcement within the circuit, such that no party can produce a valid zero-knowledge proof yielding a matching scope commitment without holding a credential commitment that is Merkle-included in the second Merkle tree, without revealing the AI agent's credential fields
rationale: This tightens Claim 1(c) to define identity-binding operationally through the ZK circuit's dual constraints (Merkle membership + hash pre-image knowledge) rather than relying on the now-contradicted definitional predicate about hash irreproducibility. The claim itself now specifies what makes the commitment 'identity-bound' — it is the conjunction of Merkle inclusion and hash pre-image knowledge enforced within the proof circuit — making the claim self-contained and not dependent on the problematic Section 3.7 definition alone.
tradeoffs: Claim 1(c) becomes significantly longer and more complex, which increases prosecution burden. The narrowing to circuit-enforced Merkle membership could allow design-arounds where the Merkle constraint is enforced outside the ZK circuit (e.g., via a separate on-chain check).

alice_101.name: alice_101
alice_101.evidence: The revised language recites specific technical mechanisms: Poseidon hash pre-image constraints, Merkle membership proof computed within the ZK circuit, bit-by-bit enforcement of permission bitmask scope policy. These are concrete cryptographic operations tied to specific circuit constraints, not abstract ideas applied on a computer. The dual-constraint structure (Merkle inclusion AND hash pre-image knowledge enforced simultaneously within the proof circuit) describes a specific machine transformation.
alice_101.critique: Still vulnerable to characterization as 'organizing human activity' (authorization/delegation) with generic cryptographic tools as the 'something more.' The Poseidon hash, Merkle tree, and ZK circuit are individually WURC in the blockchain identity space. However, the specific conjunction — circuit-internal enforcement of both Merkle membership and bitmask policy on the same hash pre-image simultaneously — is sufficiently concrete to survive Step 2 under Berkheimer. Not quite 19-20 because an examiner could still frame the core concept as 'verifying that an agent has permission before acting' which is a fundamental economic practice.

obviousness_103.name: obviousness_103
obviousness_103.evidence: Requires combining at minimum: (1) Semaphore v4 for Poseidon-based Merkle membership proofs in ZK circuits, (2) UCAN/Biscuit for permission bitmask attenuation concepts, (3) Iden3 for credential commitments in Merkle trees with nullifiers, and (4) the specific integration pattern of enforcing both Merkle membership and bitmask policy on a single Poseidon hash pre-image within one circuit. No single reference teaches identity-bound scope commitments where the binding is enforced by dual circuit constraints.
obviousness_103.critique: The motivation to combine is not terribly strained. A PHOSITA working on privacy-preserving agent authorization would naturally look to Semaphore for Merkle membership, UCAN for permission narrowing, and would recognize that combining them inside a single ZK circuit is the obvious way to get privacy. The specific choice of Poseidon hash over the conjunction of (credential_commitment, bitmask) is a straightforward engineering decision once you decide to do permission delegation in ZK. Tornado Cash Nova already teaches chained Poseidon commitments in ZK proofs. The 'identity-bound' aspect — tying Merkle membership to the hash pre-image within the same circuit — is the strongest differentiator but could be argued as an obvious optimization to prevent commitment forgery.

support_112.name: support_112
support_112.evidence: The revised claim language is self-contained and operationally defines 'identity-bound' through the circuit's dual constraints rather than relying on the problematic Section 3.7 definitional predicate. The claim specifies exactly what makes the commitment identity-bound: (i) Merkle membership of the credential commitment component, and (ii) bitmask policy satisfaction via bit-by-bit enforcement. Both mechanisms are standard Circom circuit patterns that are well within the spec's enablement.
support_112.critique: The claim is significantly longer and more complex, which creates some Nautilus definiteness risk around phrases like 'such that no party can produce a valid zero-knowledge proof yielding a matching scope commitment without holding a credential commitment that is Merkle-included.' This is a functional/negative limitation that approaches means-plus-function territory without using that format. However, the underlying cryptographic guarantee (soundness of the ZK proof system) provides the technical basis. The spec must contain worked examples of the specific Circom circuit constraints for bit-by-bit enforcement — if Section 3.7 has the inconsistency noted, prosecution will face 112(a) written description challenges even though the claim itself is now better defined.

design_around.name: design_around
design_around.evidence: The candidate itself acknowledges the key design-around: 'The narrowing to circuit-enforced Merkle membership could allow design-arounds where the Merkle constraint is enforced outside the ZK circuit (e.g., via a separate on-chain check).' A competitor could split the verification: prove permission bitmask satisfaction inside the ZK circuit but verify Merkle membership via a separate on-chain Merkle proof or an accumulator check outside the SNARK. This breaks the 'simultaneously constraining both' requirement.
design_around.critique: Beyond the acknowledged escape, a competitor could: (1) use a non-Poseidon algebraic hash (Rescue, Griffin, Anemoi) for the scope commitment — the claim says 'Poseidon hash' specifically; (2) use a vector commitment or RSA accumulator instead of a Merkle tree for credential set membership; (3) enforce identity-binding via a separate linked proof (e.g., proof-carrying data or recursive SNARK) rather than dual constraints within a single circuit. The narrowing to 'within the circuit' is operationally precise but commercially narrow — hours to days of engineering to move Merkle verification to a wrapper contract while keeping the ZK proof for bitmask privacy.

scope.name: scope
scope.evidence: The claim covers the primary product topology: a ZK circuit that simultaneously enforces Merkle membership and permission bitmask policy on a Poseidon hash pre-image. This captures the core IdentityOS mutual authentication flow where agents prove scoped credentials.
scope.critique: The scope is narrowed significantly by specifying 'Poseidon hash,' 'Merkle tree,' 'bit-by-bit enforcement,' and 'within the circuit.' This excludes: (a) implementations using other ZK-friendly hashes, (b) implementations using polynomial commitments or accumulators instead of Merkle trees, (c) implementations where Merkle verification happens in a separate linked proof or on-chain, (d) future recursive SNARK approaches where the constraint structure spans multiple circuits. The claim covers the shipped product and close variants but not the broader design space of identity-bound scope commitments. A dependent claim family would be needed to recapture scope on alternative hash functions and membership proof structures.

original_language: The method of claim 1, wherein the identity-bound scope commitment output by the second zero-knowledge proof is computed as a two-input Poseidon hash of the AI agent's permission bitmask and the AI agent's credential commitment, binding permission scope to agent identity to prevent impersonation in delegation chains.
claim_text: The method of claim 1, wherein the identity-bound scope commitment output by the second zero-knowledge proof is computed as a two-input Poseidon hash of the AI agent's permission bitmask and the AI agent's credential commitment, and wherein the scope commitment is identity-bound because the second zero-knowledge proof circuit constrains the credential commitment used as an input to said Poseidon hash to be simultaneously a leaf whose Merkle membership is proven within the circuit against the second Merkle tree, such that impersonation in delegation chains is prevented not by the infeasibility of offline hash reproduction but by the requirement that any subsequent delegation prover must produce a valid zero-knowledge proof demonstrating both knowledge of the Poseidon hash pre-image and Merkle inclusion of the credential commitment component in the on-chain agent credential tree.
rationale: This narrows Claim 5 to explicitly locate the anti-impersonation guarantee in the ZK proof's Merkle constraint rather than in hash pre-image secrecy. By stating that prevention comes 'not by the infeasibility of offline hash reproduction but by the requirement that any subsequent delegation prover must produce a valid zero-knowledge proof,' the claim language is fully consistent with Section 3.8's disclosure of 2^64 enumeration. This dependent claim becomes the fallback position if broader claim language is challenged.
tradeoffs: Explicitly conceding that offline hash reproduction is feasible in the claim language could be cited by defendants to argue the scope commitment alone has no security value. The claim now depends entirely on the ZK proof enforcement mechanism, narrowing the protection to systems that use ZK proofs for chain-linking rather than any system using identity-bound hash commitments.

alice_101.name: alice_101
alice_101.evidence: Claim recites specific two-input Poseidon hash with defined operands (permission bitmask, credential commitment), Merkle membership proof constrained within a ZK circuit against a named on-chain tree, and a circuit constraint requiring simultaneous pre-image knowledge and Merkle inclusion. This is specific machine-transformation language tied to circuit constraints, not result-oriented functional claiming.
alice_101.critique: Strong on 101 because the claim describes what the circuit actually constrains, not what it achieves in the abstract. The explicit 'not by hash pre-image infeasibility but by ZK proof requirement' language is technically precise and anchors the claim to a concrete enforcement mechanism. An examiner would have difficulty characterizing this as 'apply it on a computer' when the claim defines specific constraint relationships within an arithmetic circuit.

obviousness_103.name: obviousness_103
obviousness_103.evidence: Requires combining at minimum: (1) Semaphore v4 for Poseidon-based Merkle membership in ZK circuits, (2) UCAN/Biscuit for permission-scope binding concepts, (3) Tornado Cash Nova for chained Poseidon commitments with nullifiers, (4) the novel integration insight that anti-impersonation derives from the ZK proof's simultaneous Merkle constraint on the credential commitment component rather than from hash pre-image secrecy. No single reference teaches binding a permission bitmask and credential commitment in a two-input hash where the credential commitment must simultaneously satisfy Merkle inclusion.
obviousness_103.critique: While individual primitives are well-known, the specific integration pattern — identity-binding via dual-purpose credential commitment that must satisfy both Poseidon pre-image and Merkle leaf constraints simultaneously — requires at least 3-4 references. However, a skilled artisan working on ZKP delegation systems would have motivation to combine Semaphore-style membership proofs with UCAN-style permission binding. The motivation isn't trivial but also isn't deeply strained. KSR's 'obvious to try' argument could apply given the finite number of ways to bind identity to permissions in a ZK context.

support_112.name: support_112
support_112.evidence: The claim explicitly reconciles with Section 3.8's disclosure that 2^64 enumeration makes offline hash reproduction feasible, by locating the security guarantee in the ZK proof enforcement mechanism rather than hash secrecy. All terms are defined: 'identity-bound scope commitment,' 'two-input Poseidon hash,' 'permission bitmask,' 'credential commitment,' 'second Merkle tree.' The claim language matches the spec's circuit architecture.
support_112.critique: This is the claim's strongest dimension. By explicitly stating where the security guarantee does NOT come from (hash pre-image infeasibility) and where it does (ZK proof with Merkle constraint), the claim language is fully consistent with the specification's own security analysis. An examiner cannot attack this under Ariad for failing to disclose what the inventor knew about the hash enumeration weakness, because the claim embraces it. Definiteness is solid under Nautilus — terms are precise and anchored to cryptographic operations.

design_around.name: design_around
design_around.evidence: Escape routes: (1) Use a three-input hash instead of two-input Poseidon (add a salt/nonce as third input) — hours of circuit redesign. (2) Substitute Rescue, Griffin, or Anemoi for Poseidon — days of circuit work but architecturally identical. (3) Use BBS+ credential presentation instead of Merkle-based ZK membership — different paradigm, ~1 week. (4) Bind identity via polynomial commitment (KZG) rather than Poseidon hash — escapes the 'two-input Poseidon hash' limitation. The claim's specificity to two-input Poseidon and the explicit concession that hash reproduction is feasible both narrow the enforcement perimeter.
design_around.critique: The 'two-input Poseidon hash' specificity is a double-edged sword. It helps on 101/112 but creates a clear design-around path: any competitor who uses Poseidon with three inputs (adding a random blinding factor) or who uses a different ZK-friendly hash function escapes literal infringement. The explicit concession about hash reproduction feasibility means a competitor could argue that using the same commitment structure without ZK proofs (e.g., with TEE attestation instead) is non-infringing. Doctrine of equivalents might save some of these, but the claim language actively narrows the equivalents range.

scope.name: scope
scope.evidence: This is a dependent claim narrowed to a specific implementation: two-input Poseidon hash, specific operand pair, Merkle membership in the agent credential tree, ZK proof enforcement. It covers the primary product's delegation mechanism but explicitly concedes that the scope commitment 'alone has no security value' (per tradeoffs). The claim does not reach alternative commitment schemes, different hash arities, non-ZK enforcement mechanisms, or TEE-based alternatives.
scope.critique: As a fallback dependent claim, it serves its strategic purpose — providing a defensible position if broader claims are invalidated. But its commercial coverage is narrow: it captures only systems using this exact Poseidon(bitmask, commitment) + Merkle construction with ZK enforcement. A competitor building a similar product with BBS+ credentials, KZG commitments, or even the same Poseidon approach with a third input would not infringe. The explicit concession in the claim language ('not by the infeasibility of offline hash reproduction') further constrains scope by inviting defendants to argue that any non-ZK-enforced variant is outside the claim.

original_language:
(b) receiving, from the human user, a first zero-knowledge proof generated using a first zero-knowledge proving system, the first zero-knowledge proof proving Merkle membership of the human user's identity commitment in the first Merkle tree, computing a nullifier as a Poseidon hash of a scope identifier and a secret scalar, and computing a nonce binding as a Poseidon hash of the nullifier and a session nonce, without revealing the human user's identity;
(c) receiving, from the AI agent, a second zero-knowledge proof generated using a second zero-knowledge proving system, the second zero-knowledge proof proving Merkle membership of the AI agent's credential commitment in the second Merkle tree, verifying an EdDSA signature of an operator over the credential commitment, performing Num2Bits(64) range checks on the permission bitmask and an expiry timestamp to prevent field overflow, enforcing bit-by-bit that the permission bitmask satisfies a required scope policy, verifying the credential has not expired via a LessThan(64) comparator, and outputting, as a public signal, an identity-bound scope commitment computed as a Poseidon hash of the permission bitmask and the AI agent's credential commitment that is Merkle-included in the second Merkle tree, without revealing the AI agent's credential fields;
claim_text:
(b) receiving, from the human user, a first zero-knowledge proof generated using at least one zero-knowledge proving system, the first zero-knowledge proof proving Merkle membership of the human user's identity commitment in the first Merkle tree, computing a nullifier as a Poseidon hash of a scope identifier and a secret scalar, and computing a nonce binding as a Poseidon hash of the nullifier and a session nonce, without revealing the human user's identity;
